name: Unison
summary: Content-addressed functional language using *abilities* and *ability handlers* for effects. A function type may require an ability set, and handlers provide abilities in scope. The effect-system design is based on Frank's bidirectional discipline; the persistence model is content-addressed code (every term has a hash, not a name in a file).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Unison uses *abilities* and *ability handlers*: a function type may require an ability set, and handlers provide abilities in scope. The effect-system design is based on Frank's bidirectional discipline (`data/frank-language`); programmers do not write effect variables in source — abilities propagate structurally through type checking.

The persistence model is the more famous design point: every term in a Unison codebase is identified by a *hash* of its content, not by a path-and-name. Modules and namespaces map names to hashes, and the storage substrate is canonical (`data/unison-codebase`). The combination of content-addressed storage with abilities-driven effects is unusual and closes the loop between language design and tooling: code can be moved, renamed, or merged without changing its identity.

sections.title: Abilities versus Koka effect rows
sections.content:
Unison's abilities differ from Koka effect rows in that the source language has no syntax for effect-row variables. Like Frank, the propagation is bidirectional: an ability required by an operation flows up the call stack until a handler provides it, and the type checker confirms the chain is consistent without needing the programmer to write `<state<S>|e>` explicitly.

The trade-off: Unison source is cleaner without effect-row noise, but error messages must be careful to expose the inferred ability set when bidirectional propagation fails.
